summary: O'Neil proposes two ideas to make math research more engaging and collaborative. First, she suggests mathematicians adopt the Royal Statistical Society's tradition of holding public discussions about papers, where experts debate a paper and the author responds, with the entire conversation published. Second, she proposes using NLP techniques to cluster math papers by topic, going beyond simple citation graphs to find related work through textual analysis. She acknowledges the organizing challenges but argues both ideas would reduce the isolation mathematicians often feel and help researchers find relevant work more easily.
key_points: The Royal Statistical Society has a long tradition of formal public paper discussions that mathematics could adopt
Finding people reading the same math papers is rare and requires significant effort to organize
Citation graphs miss papers on similar topics; NLP-based clustering could find related work through titles, abstracts, and introductions
Mathematicians' love of distinctive terminology (like 'Flabby Sheaves') makes NLP techniques particularly well-suited to the field
Cross-field discovery remains challenging because different disciplines often name the same concepts differently
money_quotes: One thing that kind of depressed me about math is how rarely you'd find people reading the same papers unless you specifically got a group of people together to do so, which was a lot of work.
Right now the most obvious way to find a 'nearby' paper is to look at the graph of papers by direct reference, but you're probably missing out on lots of stuff that way.
The good news here is that mathematicians are in love with terminology, and give weird names to things that make NLP techniques very happy.
My favorite recent example which I hear Johan muttering under his breath from time to time is Flabby Sheaves. There's no way that's not a distinctive phrase.

Such techniques won't help at all in finding different fields who have come across the same idea but have different names for the relevant objects. But that's OK, because it means there's still lots of work for mathematicians.
key_insight: Mathematics research would benefit from borrowing collaborative practices from other fields—both social (public paper discussions) and technological (NLP-based paper discovery)—to combat the isolation inherent in specialized research.
